mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Iceland on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe (officially). But Iceland actually sits ON the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, where the European and North American tectonic plates pull apart. It is the only place on Earth where you can walk between two continents on land 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Iceland?
mapQuestions.answer: Reykjavík, the northernmost capital city in the world. About two-thirds of all Icelanders live in or around it.

mapQuestions.question: What language do Icelanders speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Icelandic. It has changed so little in 1,000 years that modern Icelanders can still read the original Viking sagas. Most people also speak excellent English.

mapQuestions.question: How many active volcanoes does Iceland have?
mapQuestions.answer: About 30 active ones (and around 130 total). Iceland sits on a hotspot AND a tectonic ridge, which is why volcanoes erupt here every few years.

mapQuestions.question: How many people live in Iceland?
mapQuestions.answer: About 380,000, fewer than the population of Coventry! Half of the country is uninhabited highland and glacier.

foods.name: Plokkfiskur
foods.description: A creamy mashed-fish stew of haddock or cod with potatoes, onions and white sauce. Comforting cold-weather food, served with thick rye bread and butter.

foods.name: Pylsa
foods.description: The famous Icelandic hot dog: lamb, pork and beef, with crispy fried onions, raw onions, sweet brown mustard, ketchup, and remoulade. Even Bill Clinton famously loved one in Reykjavík.

foods.name: Rúgbrauð
foods.description: Dark, sweet, dense rye bread, traditionally baked UNDERGROUND for 24 hours using geothermal heat from hot springs. Served with butter and smoked fish.

foods.name: Harðfiskur
foods.description: Wind-dried fish, usually cod or haddock, torn into salty chewy strips and eaten with butter. The original Viking snack, packed with protein, eaten for 1,000+ years.

foods.name: Skyr
foods.description: Looks like Greek yogurt but is actually a fresh cheese. Thick, mild, high-protein. Icelanders have been making it since the Vikings arrived. Brilliant for breakfast with berries.

foods.name: Kleinur
foods.description: Twisted little fried doughnuts with a crisp outside and soft inside. The traditional Sunday treat with a cup of coffee.

scavengerItems.label: An active volcano or a freshly cooled lava field (black, ropy, alien-looking)
A geyser shooting hot water into the air (Strokkur erupts every 6–10 minutes!)
A waterfall (Iceland has thousands, Skógafoss, Gullfoss, Seljalandsfoss)
An "elf rock", a stone Icelanders genuinely believe is home to hidden people, and re-route roads to avoid
A small fluffy Icelandic sheep on a hillside (there are more sheep than people!)
An Icelandic horse, short, shaggy, with FIVE gaits instead of four
The Northern Lights dancing across the sky (winter only, but you might get lucky)
A glacier, ice older than the pyramids
People bathing outdoors in steaming hot water in the middle of snow
Rain, sun, wind AND hail in the same afternoon (Icelandic weather changes every 10 minutes)
A whale spouting off the coast (Húsavík is the whale-watching capital)
A puffin colony with hundreds of nesting birds (May–August only)

geoMatches.description: The northernmost capital on Earth. Brightly painted tin-roofed houses, the rocket-shaped Hallgrímskirkja church on a hill, and the world's biggest concentration of bookshops per person.
A loop drivable from Reykjavík in one day: Þingvellir (the canyon between two continents and the world's oldest parliament site), Gullfoss waterfall, and Strokkur geyser.
A milky pale-blue geothermal pool sitting in a sharp black lava field. The water is heated naturally by the volcano underneath and is rich in silica.
Europe's biggest glacier, covering 8% of the WHOLE country. You can ride a snowmobile across it in summer or walk into blue ice caves under it in winter.
The iceberg lagoon. Blue chunks of glacier float out into the Atlantic, and the "Diamond Beach" right next door is dotted with stranded ice on black volcanic sand.
The remote north-west region of cliffs, near-empty roads, the spectacular tiered Dynjandi waterfall, and the largest puffin colony in the country.

senses.smell: Sulphur from a hot spring (a bit like boiled eggs!), fresh Atlantic sea air on a black beach, cold wet moss after rain, geothermal-baked rye bread, woodsmoke from a turf-roofed mountain hut…
senses.hear: Wind, always wind, on a glacier or a beach, water gurgling underground before a geyser shoots, sheep bleating on a hillside, Icelandic that sounds like a softer Norse language, the silence of a snowy plateau, distant whales blowing…
senses.taste: Salty wind-dried fish, sweet creamy skyr with berries, the strange dark sweetness of geothermal rye bread, hot Icelandic hot-dog mustard, ice-cream eaten outdoors in a snowstorm (Icelanders really do that!)…
senses.feel: Stinging Atlantic wind on your face, hot 38°C geothermal water meeting freezing 2°C air on your shoulders, soft fluffy Icelandic wool jumpers, ancient black volcanic sand under bare feet, the deep silence of a glacier…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why do Icelanders queue for hot pools?
stories.body: Every town in Iceland, every single one, even tiny ones, has a public "sundlaug": an outdoor swimming pool heated by geothermal water from the rocks below. They are open all year. People go in the snow, in the rain, in the dark of winter, every day. The pool is where you catch up with neighbours, where teenagers hang out, where business deals get sealed in the "heitur pottur" (hot tub). The geothermal water comes up out of the ground at 80°C or hotter, gets piped into the pool, and never needs heating with electricity. It is one of the reasons Iceland's heating bills are so low, about 90% of homes are heated entirely with geothermal water.

stories.question: Do Icelanders really believe in elves?
stories.body: About half of Icelanders, when asked, say they at least don't rule out the existence of elves and "huldufólk" (hidden people). When a road is being built and a known "elf rock" is in the way, the road often gets re-routed around the rock instead of moved. There are court cases about it. There are construction delays about it. There is a special "elf school" in Reykjavík you can sign up to. Belief in hidden people goes back at least 1,000 years, to the original Norse settlers. It is part of why Icelanders so deeply respect the land, every lava field, hill and stream might have someone living in it.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A whole road might be moved around a rock because of an old story. Is that silly, or is it actually a really good way to treat the land?

stories.question: Who first lived in Iceland?
stories.body: Iceland was one of the last places on Earth to be settled by humans. A Norse Viking called Ingólfr Arnarson is traditionally said to have built the first farm here in 874 AD, in what is now Reykjavík. His ship's wooden pillars washed ashore on the bay; he took it as a sign and built his home there. For the next few centuries, Iceland became a country of independent farmers, with the world's oldest still-running parliament, the "Alþingi", meeting outdoors in a giant rift valley called Þingvellir from 930 AD onwards. The Vikings' descendants are today's Icelanders, and a lot of families can still trace their ancestry back to specific Viking settlers from the 800s.

stories.question: How is Iceland still growing?
stories.body: Iceland sits exactly on the join between the North American and Eurasian tectonic plates. Those plates are pulling apart by about 2.5 cm per year. As they pull apart, magma from deep inside the Earth pushes up through the crack, builds new rock on the surface, and adds it to the country. That's how Iceland was made in the first place: it is literally a piece of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge that grew tall enough to poke above the sea. At Þingvellir you can walk along a canyon that is the edge of the European plate, look across, and see the edge of the North American plate on the other side. The country grows wider, year after year, forever.

stories.question: Why has the Icelandic language barely changed in 1,000 years?
stories.body: Most languages drift fast, English from 1,000 years ago is barely recognisable to a modern English speaker. But Icelandic, written down by Viking settlers in the 800s and 900s, is still almost completely readable to modern Icelanders. Why? Iceland was isolated, sparsely populated, and stayed that way for a thousand years. There was no big neighbour pushing French or German or Latin in. The original literature, the Sagas, written in the 1200s, stayed alive as bedtime stories, schoolbooks and family pride. Today the Icelandic Language Committee invents new Icelandic words for modern things instead of borrowing from English: a computer is "tölva" (a number-prophetess), and a telephone is "sími" (an old word for "thread"). It is one of the most preserved languages on Earth.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A country can deliberately keep its language alive by inventing new words instead of borrowing them. Would you do that, or would you just borrow from English?

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Iceland so far?
Have you tried saying "takk" to anyone? What did they say back?
Volcanoes, glaciers, geysers, hot pools, which has felt the most "Iceland"?
Did you get into a public hot pool? How quickly did you stop noticing the cold air?
If you could spot ONE Icelandic animal up close, which would it be, puffin, whale, Icelandic horse, or arctic fox?
Iceland's language has barely changed in 1,000 years. Why do you think English has changed so much in the same time?

wordSearch.hint: Icelandic foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Icelandic sheep
animals.description: Stocky, double-coated, often horned. Roam freely on hillsides all summer. There are more sheep than people in Iceland.

animals.name: Icelandic horse
animals.description: Short, shaggy, brought by the Vikings 1,000+ years ago, and never crossbred since. Has FIVE gaits instead of four.

animals.name: Atlantic puffin
animals.description: Half of the world's Atlantic puffins nest in Iceland. Best places: the Westman Islands and Látrabjarg cliffs, May to August.

animals.name: White-tailed eagle
animals.description: Iceland's biggest bird of prey, with a 2.5 m wingspan. About 80 breeding pairs left, mostly in the Westfjords.

animals.name: Humpback whale
animals.description: Húsavík in the north is the whale-watching capital of Europe. Humpbacks breach right next to the boats.

animals.name: Arctic fox
animals.description: Iceland's only native land mammal, here since the Ice Age. Pure white in winter, brown in summer.
